text: Today we are presenting a laparoscopic total gastrectomy and omentectomy. The authors have no disclosures. The patient is a sixty-one year old male who presented with dysphagia, weight loss, and anemia. He was referred for investigation of microcytic anemia. Gastroscopy revealed a gastric mass and a biopsy showed a gastric adenocarcinoma. CT and PET scan showed no metastatic disease.
Six laparoscopic ports are visibly placed in the abdominal wall, arranged according to the surgical diagram for optimal access during the gastrectomy procedure.
The surgeon dissects the omentum from the transverse colon, starting at the hepatic flexure and progressing towards the splenic flexure, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon divides the left gastric artery using a vascular stapler. The right gastric artery is dissected and clipped, followed by the division of the right gastroepiploic artery with a vascular stapler. The pylorus is identified, and the duodenum is divided using the stapler.

The surgeon applies a suture to the jejunum to mark the Roux limb, ensuring correct anastomosis. The jejunum is divided 40 centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz, and the mesentery is divided using a vascular stapler. A jejuno-jejunostomy is performed with two staplers.
Three marking sutures are applied to the jejunum, which is then fixed to the abdominal wall to establish a feeding jejunostomy.
The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus within the mediastinum and transects it using a stapler. Two traction sutures are applied on each sidewall of the esophagus to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon brings up the roux limb to perform a stapled esophago-jejunostomy by opening the jejunum. The completeness of the anastomosis is verified by checking the donuts, and the jejunal defect is closed with a stapler.
The surgeon closes the hiatus by approximating the left and right crura, followed by the insertion of a surgical drain into the area.
The surgeon removes the gastrectomy specimen through an enlarged port, with clear margins visible on the excised tissue.

The patient remained stable throughout his recovery. He started J-tube feedings on postoperative day three. On postoperative day five, he underwent a barium swallow study, which showed no leak of his surgical anastomosis. On postoperative day six, he was tolerating J-tube feedings and full fluid diet, and all of his drains were removed. On postoperative day seven, the patient was discharged home.
The pathological staging was a T three and two M zero stage three A.
